evals.prompt: hey so tourism authority asked us to dig into this. my director keeps saying 'our recovery is done, visitor arrivals are back to normal' but the regional offices are complaining. the data is monthly visitor arrivals by island for 2024 and 2025, broken out by accommodation type, tier, and origin market (it's tourism_monthly.csv). can you put together a markdown report i can share with the director - what's actually going on across the islands and what should we do differently. assume the director knows the business but doesn't look at data much.
evals.expected_output: A markdown report (report.md) with the six-phase analysis surfacing that the aggregate is flat BUT Maui is growing while Oahu is declining (Simpson's Paradox), with root-cause drill-down identifying Oahu hotel luxury tier (especially Japanese market) as the driver. Includes action-titled charts, a CTR narrative, and ranked recommendations with owners and confidence grades.
expectations: A markdown report file (report.md) exists as the final deliverable
Report surfaces Simpson's Paradox: aggregate is roughly flat while islands diverge
Report identifies Maui as the growth outlier AND Oahu as the decline driver
Root-cause drill-down reaches at least 3 layers deep
Report contains at least 3 charts (PNGs embedded or referenced)
At least 2 chart titles are action-oriented
Report follows CTR narrative structure
Recommendations are ranked with owners AND confidence grades
Arithmetic was independently validated
Tone is appropriate for a director audience

evals.prompt: customer churn spiked in q2 2026 and the board wants answers at next week's meeting. i have customer-level data in customer_churn.csv with plan tier, industry, signup and churn quarters, ARR, tenure. i need a marp slide deck - action titles, not more than 15 slides, with a clear ask at the end. the board is non-technical. what's driving the churn and what do we do about it?
evals.expected_output: A Marp slide deck (deck.md) where the analysis shows churn rise is concentrated in Pro plan + Retail industry (drill-down), NOT systemic. Deck has 10-15 slides with action titles, CTR narrative, and recommendations targeting Pro+Retail segment specifically. Validated via re-derivation of churn rates.
expectations: A Marp deck file (deck.md) exists with valid Marp frontmatter
Deck has 15 or fewer slides
Analysis identifies Pro plan tier as the primary driver
Drill-down identifies Retail industry within Pro as the top churn segment
Churn rates are independently re-derived
At least 2 slide titles are action-oriented
Deck follows CTR narrative structure
Recommendations are specific, named, with confidence grades
At least 3 charts included as PNGs
Tone is non-technical and appropriate for a board audience

evals.prompt: our marketing team keeps saying conversion is 'flat' this quarter and nothing to worry about. gut says something's off - we've been spending more on paid and organic traffic is up. data is funnel_monthly.csv (jan-jun 2026) with visits, signups, conversions by channel and user_type (new vs returning). please build me a jupyter notebook i can share with the growth team so they can re-run it. tell me whether conversion is actually flat and what's happening underneath.
evals.expected_output: A Jupyter notebook (analysis.ipynb) revealing that aggregate conversion is ~flat BUT new-user conversion improved ~5% → ~7% while returning-user conversion regressed from ~14% → ~10%. Notebook has reproducible cells, CTR narrative in markdown, action-titled charts, and recommendations.
expectations: A valid Jupyter notebook file (analysis.ipynb) exists as the final deliverable
Notebook confirms aggregate conversion is roughly flat
Notebook surfaces that new-user conversion improved
Notebook surfaces that returning-user conversion regressed
Notebook explicitly names the pattern as Simpson's Paradox OR composition shift
Notebook code cells are reproducible
Notebook has narrative markdown cells
At least 2 chart titles are action-oriented
At least 3 charts are included
Recommendations included with owners and confidence grades
